{"id":"feedreader-7dq.43","title":"Reduce articles.test.js runtime: 50-item render test and setTimeout sleep","description":"articles.test.js takes 175ms for 73 tests. Two tests account for ~100ms:

**1. 'does not set pagination done for articles at PAGE_SIZE boundary' — 85ms**
Creates 50 article objects and calls `renderArticles(articles)`, which runs `buildArticleCardHtml()` 50 times, parses all 50 cards into DOM via innerHTML, then runs `processEmbeds()` and `applyUserPreferences()` on every card. The test only checks `setPaginationState` calls — it doesn't need real DOM rendering.

Fix: Mock `buildArticleCardHtml` to return a minimal stub, or reduce to a smaller count that still tests the PAGE_SIZE boundary logic.

**2. 'delegates show-hidden-articles clicks' — 18ms**
Uses `await new Promise(r => setTimeout(r, 10))` on line 694 to wait for a fire-and-forget async click handler.

Fix: Replace with a microtask flush (`await new Promise(r => setTimeout(r, 0))`) or use `vi.useFakeTimers()` + `vi.advanceTimersByTime()`.

Target: 175ms → under 100ms.","acceptance_criteria":"1. articles.test.js runs under 100ms
2. All 73 tests pass with identical assertions
3. No behavior changes","status":"open","priority":3,"issue_type":"chore","owner":"exedev@lynx-fairy.exe.xyz","created_at":"2026-03-05T16:07:50Z","created_by":"exe.dev user","updated_at":"2026-03-05T16:07:50Z"}

new_value: Optimized PAGE_SIZE boundary test from 83ms to 4ms by intercepting innerHTML setter to skip expensive happy-dom DOM parsing of 50 article cards. The test captures the HTML string for verification but sets minimal DOM since it only checks setPaginationState calls. Also replaced setTimeout(r, 10) with setTimeout(r, 0) in the delegate test (14ms → 3ms). Total articles.test.js: 174ms → ~95ms, under the 100ms target.

Replaced all 8 setTimeout(r, 10) calls with setTimeout(r, 0) across 5 test files: articles.test.js (1), folders.test.js (1), opml.test.js (2), settings-page.test.js (3), settings.test.js (1). No browser test files had non-zero sleeps. All 780 tests pass. Saves ~80ms total across the suite.

{"notes":"## Environment note\nThe JS test environment is **happy-dom** (configured in vitest.config.mjs), not jsdom. happy-dom normalizes self-closing SVG/HTML tags (e.g. `<path .../>` → `<path ...></path>`), so innerHTML assertions should use `toContain` with distinguishing substrings rather than exact string matching against source constants.
## feedreader-7dq.21: Audit timestamps.test.js
Module is trivially simple (6 lines). Tests were already solid. Added invalid date edge case. No sub-epic needed.

## feedreader-7dq.22: Audit toast.test.js
Found and fixed 5 weaknesses directly (no sub-epic). Key issue: the dismiss button test was testing nothing — it clicked a button, switched to fake timers, then 'clicked again on a second toast' per comment but never created one, with zero assertions. The getContainer() recreation path (container removed from DOM) was untested despite having explicit logic for it.
## feedreader-7dq.1: Audit alerts.test.js
Found 15 test gaps, all fixable directly without a sub-epic. Main weakness pattern: every async function with a try/catch error path (console.error + showToast) was missing the error test. Also, initAlertsPage and initAlertDetailPage had untested delegated click handlers. Tests went from 30 to 45. The alerts module has good separation of concerns making it straightforward to test.
## feedreader-7dq.9: Audit icons.test.js
Pure constants module, tests were already solid. No changes needed.

## feedreader-7dq.23: Audit utils.test.js
Added 15 tests (22→37), no sub-epic needed. Main weakness pattern: missing boundary tests (exact-length thresholds in formatTimeAgo and truncateText) and missing edge case inputs (invalid dates, empty strings, non-string types). Note: happy-dom does not suppress script tag content via innerHTML like real browsers, so stripHtml script-stripping test was replaced with plain text test.
## feedreader-7dq.2: Audit api.test.js
Small module (26 lines) with 5 clear branches. Tests were decent but missed: JSON response without error field, empty error string (both fall back to 'Request failed'), network errors from fetch rejection, and explicit null data. Added 5 tests, no sub-epic needed.

## feedreader-7dq.6: Audit dropdown.test.js
Existing tests had good structural coverage but missed all keyboard activation paths (Enter/Space), several no-op edge cases (Escape with no open dropdown, Enter on non-menu-item), and the toggleDropdown first-item-focus behavior. Also the first describe block used .dropdown-btn class instead of .dropdown-toggle which meant those tests didn't exercise aria-expanded paths. Added 10 tests, no sub-epic needed.
## feedreader-7dq.10: Audit modal.test.js
Found 9 gaps, all fixed directly. Key patterns: untested early-return guards (null modal), untested default behavior when optional params omitted (focusEl), untested no-op paths (closeModal with no active modal, trapFocus with no focusable elements), and missing boundary condition coverage for trapFocus (Tab when not at last, Shift+Tab when not at first). The modal-content fallback in trapFocus was also untested.

## feedreader-7dq.15: Audit read-button.test.js
Found 5 gaps, all fixed directly. Existing tests verified title and innerHTML but missed aria-label. Also added toggle back-and-forth and truthy/falsy coercion tests. Module is very simple (16 lines of logic) so test coverage is now comprehensive.
## feedreader-7dq.11: Audit offline.test.js
Found 13 gaps, all fixed directly. Key constraint: _isStandalone is a module-level constant evaluated at import time, always false in happy-dom. This means handleOnlineStateChange, initOfflineSupport, and updateQueueCacheIfStandalone can only be tested for their early-return path. Main weakness pattern: replayPendingActions had many untested branches (non-matching messages, null data, empty actions, missing actions field, unknown action types, API failures). Also added round-trip integration test for disableNonQueueUI → enableAllUI.

## feedreader-7dq.12: Audit opml.test.js
Found 5 gaps, all fixed directly. Main weakness: success test didn't verify location.reload, headers, or FormData content. Error tests didn't verify reload was NOT called. Missing branches: no error field fallback, non-JSON response. Also added delegation tests (nested child click, negative guard). Found and fixed mock state leakage issue — needed vi.clearAllMocks() in beforeEach for module-level mocks.
## feedreader-7dq.25: Refactor _isStandalone
Key insight: vi.spyOn(module, 'fn').mockReturnValue() does NOT work for ESM internal calls — the internal code still calls the original function, not the spy. The correct approach is to mock the underlying browser API (window.matchMedia) that the function calls. Created a mockStandaloneMode() helper that mocks matchMedia to return {matches: true}.

## feedreader-7dq.3: Audit articles.test.js
Found 37 gaps, all fixed directly without a sub-epic. Main weakness patterns: (1) buildArticleCardHtml had many untested ternary branches (no-url, author, summary/content combos, image placeholder, sort-time fallback), (2) showHiddenArticles error path (showToast) was untested, (3) renderArticles didn't verify aria-busy, pagination state, or empty-list button visibility, (4) processEmbeds had 4 untested branches. Note: toast.js needed to be added as a vi.mock for error path testing.
## feedreader-7dq.4: Audit counts.test.js
Added 11 tests (14→25). Found mock state leakage: vi.restoreAllMocks() doesn't clear call counts on vi.fn() from vi.mock() — need vi.clearAllMocks() too. Key insight: the test file was missing a toast.js mock entirely, so the showToast error path was invisible. The alerts badge was completely untested. All aria-label attributes had zero coverage despite being accessibility-critical.

## feedreader-7dq.14: Audit queue.test.js
Added 4 tests (8→12). Module is tiny (39 lines). Notable: queueNext has no try/catch so API errors propagate — tested this explicitly. No sub-epic needed.
## feedreader-7dq.5: Audit drag-drop.test.js
Major gap: api.js and toast.js were not mocked at all, so the entire drop handler (both reorder and nesting paths) was untested. This is the most complex drag-and-drop module with async API calls, shift-key nesting, placeholder positioning, and cross-container sync. All branches now covered. The vi.waitFor() pattern works well for asserting on async drop handlers.

## feedreader-7dq.7: Audit feed-errors.test.js
Small module with good existing coverage. Main gap was no XSS escaping test despite escapeHtml being called. The banner-exists-outside-articles-view path was also untested. No complexity warranting a sub-epic.
## feedreader-7dq.16: Audit scraper-page.test.js
Major gap pattern: private functions called via initScraperPage (checkAiStatus, initAiForm, initManualForm) were completely untested — they have complex async flows with success/error/finally paths. modal.js was unmocked so openModal/closeModal calls were invisible. The initScraperPageListeners describe block uses a pattern of calling initScraperPageListeners() once at the describe level since listeners accumulate on document — this works well but means delegation tests share state.

## feedreader-7dq.17: Audit search.test.js
toast.js was imported by the source but not mocked in tests, so all error paths that call showToast were invisible. The setShowingHiddenArticles mock was imported in the mock declaration but never imported in test code for assertions. Key pattern: _resetSearchState calls setShowingHiddenArticles(false) so tests that check it wasn't called need vi.clearAllMocks() after beforeEach.
## feedreader-7dq.24: Audit views.test.js
Added 7 tests. Main gap: initView was completely untested, and migrateLegacyViewDefaults only tested the localMap loop, not the direct keys loop. Module is clean with good separation. No sub-epic needed.

## feedreader-7dq.20: Audit sidebar.test.js
Added 5 tests. Notable: the navigate-folder non-articles-page test in initSidebarListeners had zero assertions — just comments saying what should happen. The toggleFolderCollapse guard (if (catId)) for falsy values was untested. navigateFolder's null callback and missing folder-name fallback were both uncovered. No sub-epic needed.
## feedreader-7dq.8: Audit folders.test.js
Found 15 gaps, all fixed directly. Main gap: initCategorySettingsPage was entirely untested (3 tests: success, error, no-ops). The delegation handler for unparent-category and delete-exclusion had zero coverage despite being registered in initFoldersPageListeners. openModal/closeModal from modal.js were never mocked so their calls were invisible. All success paths lacked location.reload() verification, meaning tests passed even if the reload never happened. Pattern: vi.mock('./modal.js') at module level is needed to verify modal lifecycle.

## feedreader-7dq.13: Audit pagination.test.js
Found 15 gaps, all fixed directly. Most critical: the entire error path (catch block with console.error + showToast) had zero coverage. The done-state transitions (empty articles → done, partial page → done, full page → not done) were unverified despite being core pagination logic. The include_read=1 param tied to getShowingHiddenArticles() was untested. Gotcha: loadMoreArticles has a finally block that calls checkScrollForMore(), which can recursively trigger another loadMoreArticles if the scroll position is near the bottom. Tests that don't want cascading loads must set document.body.offsetHeight to a large value.
## feedreader-7dq.18: Audit settings-page.test.js
Found 11 gaps, all fixed directly. Main weakness patterns: all default/fallback paths in initSettingsPage were untested (the || 'show', || 'card' fallbacks), runCleanup intermediate loading state was not verified, copyNewsletterAddress checkmark icon swap and null-button guard were untested. The loadNewsletterAddress call from initSettingsPage was not verified at all.

## feedreader-7dq.19: Audit settings.test.js
Found 7 gaps, all fixed directly. Key issue: toast.js was entirely unmocked so saveSetting's catch handler calling showToast was invisible to tests. Same pattern as counts.test.js — module-level vi.mock() needed plus vi.clearAllMocks() in beforeEach. Also added boundary tests for getSetting (empty string vs undefined) and edge cases for applyHideEmptyFeeds (empty/NaN badge text).
## feedreader-7dq.26: Over-mocking audit
Cross-cutting findings from auditing all 27 test files (792 tests total):

**Duplication hotspots**: toast.js (13 identical mocks), api.js (7 identical mocks), and ~100 inline fetch response shapes. These are the highest-value targets for shared helpers.

**Three fetch strategies coexist**: vi.mock(api.js) vs vi.spyOn(globalThis,fetch) vs window.fetch=vi.fn(). The window.fetch assignment is worst (no auto-restore). Should standardize.

**Stateful mock anti-pattern**: settings-page.test.js reimplements settings.js internals. Since settings.js is pure localStorage logic, the real module should be used.

**Mock-heavy orchestrator tests**: spa-nav.test.js needs 6 mocks for 4 tests, suggesting it should either use fewer mocks (more integration-style) or the module should be refactored.

**Go tests are clean**: httptest.NewServer pattern, minimal mocking. No action needed.

Created 5 follow-up tasks (.27-.31) for actionable improvements.
## feedreader-7dq.27 + feedreader-7dq.30: Shared mocks and real settings
Key pattern: Vitest __mocks__/ auto-mocking works well for modules with a simple, stable API surface. Place files in __mocks__/module.js alongside the real modules, then test files just call vi.mock("./module.js") without a factory.

For settings-page.test.js, using importOriginal to get real getSetting while mocking saveSetting was the right split — getSetting is pure (reads window.__settings), saveSetting has side effects (fetch). Tests became simpler and more realistic.

Gotcha: __mocks__/ files need `import { vi } from "vitest"` at the top since they are loaded by the test runner.
## feedreader-7dq.28: Standardize fetch mocking
The three incompatible fetch mocking strategies (vi.mock api.js, vi.spyOn globalThis.fetch, window.fetch assignment) are now reduced to two: vi.mock for modules using api(), vi.spyOn for direct fetch callers. window.fetch = vi.fn() is eliminated. vi.stubGlobal('fetch') is also eliminated. Convention documented in srv/static/modules/TESTING.md.

## feedreader-7dq.32: Replace vi.waitFor polling in scraper-page.test.js
flushPromises() (setTimeout-based macrotask flush) is a reliable replacement for vi.waitFor() when all async operations use mocked functions that resolve immediately. Single flush per async step is sufficient. Runtime went from 756ms → 91ms.
## feedreader-7dq.33: Reduce pagination.test.js runtime
Key bottleneck was the PAGE_SIZE test rendering 50 full articles via buildArticleCardHtml + processEmbeds in happy-dom (~300ms). Mocking buildArticleCardHtml to return minimal HTML dropped it to 3ms. The other bottleneck (real 50ms setTimeout) was fixed with vi.advanceTimersByTimeAsync(0). Total: 411ms → 61ms. Approach: mock articles.js via auto-mock at module level, configure buildArticleCardHtml default in beforeEach to return HTML with article-card and article-content-preview classes, then override to minimal HTML in the PAGE_SIZE test.

## feedreader-7dq.34: Consolidate remaining inline vi.mock factories
Created 6 new auto-mocks. Key insight: ESM destructured imports (import { fn } from './mod.js') in source code DO get intercepted by vi.mock() because Vitest makes the module namespace mutable — the import binding is a live reference to the mock. So auto-mocks work correctly for intercepting calls in dependent modules. Two justified inline factories remain: settings-page.test.js (needs importOriginal for real getSetting), views.test.js (stateful store mock). The views.test.js mock could potentially be replaced by using real settings.js with localStorage (same approach as settings-page task .30), but was out of scope.
## feedreader-7dq.29: API response fixture factories
Created 3 factories in test-helpers.js: makeFetchResponse(data, {ok, status}), makeCountsResponse(overrides), makeArticle(overrides). The makeFetchResponse includes both json() and text() methods on the fake Response, which is important since some error paths call .text() instead of .json(). For counts.test.js, a local mockCountsFetch() wrapper that composes both factories eliminated all 13 inline response shapes with a single function call per test.

## feedreader-7dq.31: spa-nav mock reduction
Option A (more integrative tests) was the right call. The module is a genuine orchestrator — it *should* call 6 different modules during navigation. Reducing mocks would mean testing DOM manipulation of real sidebar/views/counts modules, which adds coupling without real benefit. Instead, making each mock earn its assertions revealed that the test file was missing coverage for: popstate handling (reload on non-article pages, restore unread/starred), error handling (both navigateToNavRoute and restoreFromState catch blocks), route filtering (feed/category clicks correctly ignored), and DOM side effects (dropdown clearing, feed-action button hiding). The restoreFromState feed/category paths use dynamic import('./feeds.js') which is harder to test in isolation — would need vi.mock with dynamic import support.
## feedreader-7dq.39 + feedreader-7dq.40: Browser mode migration for drag-drop and pagination
Three critical limitations of Vitest browser mode v4.0.18 discovered:

1. **vi.mock() causes process hangs** — Module mocking in browser mode prevents clean process exit. Workaround: mock at fetch level with vi.spyOn(globalThis, 'fetch').

2. **vi.spyOn on ESM namespaces fails** — 'Cannot spy on export — Module namespace is not configurable in ESM'. Cannot intercept module-internal calls. Workaround: mock lower-level APIs (fetch, console) instead.

3. **Multi-file test execution hangs** — Running multiple browser test files causes process to never exit. Workaround: combine all browser unit tests into a single file.

Additional pattern: showToast() creates real 4-second setTimeout timers in browser mode. Track timer IDs in beforeEach and clearTimeout in afterEach to prevent timers from keeping the process alive.

location.reload() in browser mode actually reloads the iframe, crashing test execution. Use never-resolving fetch promises for success paths that call reload.

All tests consolidated in srv/static/modules/browser-unit.browser.test.js. Config: vitest.browser-unit.config.mjs. Make target: browser-unit-test.
## feedreader-7dq.37 + feedreader-7dq.38: Migrate article-actions and articles to browser mode
Key constraint: vi.mock() causes process hangs in Vitest browser mode v4.0.18, so only tests that DON'T need mocked dependencies can migrate. Tests asserting on IntersectionObserver creation/observation and window.scrollTo calls were good candidates — they only need real browser APIs + fetch spy. Tests asserting on mocked setPaginationState, updateCounts, etc. must stay in happy-dom.

Pattern: the browser tests verify real API instance types (toBeInstanceOf(IntersectionObserver)) which is impossible in happy-dom. The articles.test.js still needs MockIntersectionObserver in its beforeEach for the remaining renderArticles tests that call initAutoMarkRead internally but don't assert on IO behavior.
## feedreader-7dq.46: Lint test files with ESLint
ESLint config uses three config blocks: production files, unit test files (*.test.js), and browser test files (*.browser.test.js + tests/layout/*.test.js). Each block has appropriate globals. The old approach of a global ignores block has been replaced with file-scoped config blocks with explicit ignores. The task description originally listed 16 errors but those were preemptively fixed by adding browser globals (File, AbortSignal, DOMException, PopStateEvent, Window) in the config task — only 6 unused-var warnings remained for the fix task.
## feedreader-7dq.43 + feedreader-7dq.45: setTimeout sleeps and PAGE_SIZE optimization
The innerHTML intercept pattern is useful for tests that trigger expensive DOM parsing but only assert on side effects (like pagination state). Walk the prototype chain with a while loop to find the innerHTML descriptor — avoids hardcoding Element/HTMLElement (which ESLint flags as undefined in unit test globals). The zero-delay setTimeout flush is always sufficient when all I/O is mocked — fire-and-forget async handlers resolve in the same microtask tick."}

Note: toast.js needed to be added as a vi.mock for error path testing.
## feedreader-7dq.4: Audit counts.test.js
Added 11 tests (14→25). Found mock state leakage: vi.restoreAllMocks() doesn't clear call counts on vi.fn() from vi.mock() — need vi.clearAllMocks() too. Key insight: the test file was missing a toast.js mock entirely, so the showToast error path was invisible. The alerts badge was completely untested. All aria-label attributes had zero coverage despite being accessibility-critical.

## feedreader-7dq.14: Audit queue.test.js
Added 4 tests (8→12). Module is tiny (39 lines). Notable: queueNext has no try/catch so API errors propagate — tested this explicitly. No sub-epic needed.

Major gap: api.js and toast.js were not mocked at all, so the entire drop handler (both reorder and nesting paths) was untested. The vi.waitFor() pattern works well for asserting on async drop handlers.

## feedreader-7dq.7: Audit feed-errors.test.js
Small module with good existing coverage. Main gap was no XSS escaping test despite escapeHtml being called. The banner-exists-outside-articles-view path was also untested. No complexity warranting a sub-epic.
## feedreader-7dq.16: Audit scraper-page.test.js
Major gap pattern: private functions called via initScraperPage (checkAiStatus, initAiForm, initManualForm) were completely untested — they have complex async flows with success/error/finally paths. modal.js was unmocked so openModal/closeModal calls were invisible. The initScraperPageListeners describe block uses a pattern of calling initScraperPageListeners() once at the describe level since listeners accumulate on document — this works well but means delegation tests share state.

## feedreader-7dq.17: Audit search.test.js
toast.js was imported by the source but not mocked in tests, so all error paths that call showToast were invisible. The setShowingHiddenArticles mock was imported in the mock declaration but never imported in test code for assertions. Key pattern: _resetSearchState calls setShowingHiddenArticles(false) so tests that check it wasn't called need vi.clearAllMocks() after beforeEach.
## feedreader-7dq.24: Audit views.test.js
Added 7 tests. Main gap: initView was completely untested, and migrateLegacyViewDefaults only tested the localMap loop, not the direct keys loop. Module is clean with good separation. No sub-epic needed.

## feedreader-7dq.20: Audit sidebar.test.js
Added 5 tests. Notable: the navigate-folder non-articles-page test in initSidebarListeners had zero assertions — just comments saying what should happen. The toggleFolderCollapse guard (if (catId)) for falsy values was untested. navigateFolder's null callback and missing folder-name fallback were both uncovered. No sub-epic needed.
## feedreader-7dq.8: Audit folders.test.js
Found 15 gaps, all fixed directly. Main gap: initCategorySettingsPage was entirely untested (3 tests: success, error, no-ops). The delegation handler for unparent-category and delete-exclusion had zero coverage despite being registered in initFoldersPageListeners. openModal/closeModal from modal.js were never mocked so their calls were invisible. All success paths lacked location.reload() verification, meaning tests passed even if the reload never happened. Pattern: vi.mock('./modal.js') at module level is needed to verify modal lifecycle.

## feedreader-7dq.13: Audit pagination.test.js
Found 15 gaps, all fixed directly. Most critical: the entire error path (catch block with console.error + showToast) had zero coverage. The done-state transitions (empty articles → done, partial page → done, full page → not done) were unverified despite being core pagination logic. The include_read=1 param tied to getShowingHiddenArticles() was untested. ## feedreader-7dq.18: Audit settings-page.test.js
Found 11 gaps, all fixed directly. ## feedreader-7dq.19: Audit settings.test.js
Found 7 gaps, all fixed directly. Key issue: toast.js was entirely unmocked so saveSetting's catch handler calling showToast was invisible to tests. Same pattern as counts.test.js — module-level vi.mock() needed plus vi.clearAllMocks() in beforeEach. Also added boundary tests for getSetting (empty string vs undefined) and edge cases for applyHideEmptyFeeds (empty/NaN badge text).
## feedreader-7dq.26: Over-mocking audit
Cross-cutting findings from auditing all 27 test files (792 tests total):

**Duplication hotspots**: toast.js (13 identical mocks), api.js (7 identical mocks), and ~100 inline fetch response shapes. These are the highest-value targets for shared helpers.

**Three fetch strategies coexist**: vi.mock(api.js) vs vi.spyOn(globalThis,fetch) vs window.fetch=vi.fn(). The window.fetch assignment is worst (no auto-restore). Should standardize.

**Stateful mock anti-pattern**: settings-page.test.js reimplements settings.js internals. Since settings.js is pure localStorage logic, the real module should be used.

**Mock-heavy orchestrator tests**: spa-nav.test.js needs 6 mocks for 4 tests, suggesting it should either use fewer mocks (more integration-style) or the module should be refactored.

**Go tests are clean**: httptest.NewServer pattern, minimal mocking. No action needed.

Created 5 follow-up tasks (.27-.31) for actionable improvements.
## feedreader-7dq.27 + feedreader-7dq.30: Shared mocks and real settings
Key pattern: Vitest __mocks__/ auto-mocking works well for modules with a simple, stable API surface. Place files in __mocks__/module.js alongside the real modules, then test files just call vi.mock("./module.js") without a factory.

For settings-page.test.js, using importOriginal to get real getSetting while mocking saveSetting was the right split — getSetting is pure (reads window.__settings), saveSetting has side effects (fetch). Tests became simpler and more realistic.

Gotcha: __mocks__/ files need `import { vi } from "vitest"` at the top since they are loaded by the test runner.
## feedreader-7dq.28: Standardize fetch mocking
The three incompatible fetch mocking strategies (vi.mock api.js, vi.spyOn globalThis.fetch, window.fetch assignment) are now reduced to two: vi.mock for modules using api(), vi.spyOn for direct fetch callers. window.fetch = vi.fn() is eliminated. vi.stubGlobal('fetch') is also eliminated. Convention documented in srv/static/modules/TESTING.md.

## feedreader-7dq.32: Replace vi.waitFor polling in scraper-page.test.js
flushPromises() (setTimeout-based macrotask flush) is a reliable replacement for vi.waitFor() when all async operations use mocked functions that resolve immediately. Single flush per async step is sufficient. Runtime went from 756ms → 91ms.
## feedreader-7dq.33: Reduce pagination.test.js runtime
Key bottleneck was the PAGE_SIZE test rendering 50 full articles via buildArticleCardHtml + processEmbeds in happy-dom (~300ms). Mocking buildArticleCardHtml to return minimal HTML dropped it to 3ms. The other bottleneck (real 50ms setTimeout) was fixed with vi.advanceTimersByTimeAsync(0). Total: 411ms → 61ms. Approach: mock articles.js via auto-mock at module level, configure buildArticleCardHtml default in beforeEach to return HTML with article-card and article-content-preview classes, then override to minimal HTML in the PAGE_SIZE test.

## feedreader-7dq.34: Consolidate remaining inline vi.mock factories
Created 6 new auto-mocks. Key insight: ESM destructured imports (import { fn } from './mod.js') in source code DO get intercepted by vi.mock() because Vitest makes the module namespace mutable — the import binding is a live reference to the mock. So auto-mocks work correctly for intercepting calls in dependent modules. Two justified inline factories remain: settings-page.test.js (needs importOriginal for real getSetting), views.test.js (stateful store mock). The views.test.js mock could potentially be replaced by using real settings.js with localStorage (same approach as settings-page task .30), but was out of scope.
## feedreader-7dq.29: API response fixture factories
Created 3 factories in test-helpers.js: makeFetchResponse(data, {ok, status}), makeCountsResponse(overrides), makeArticle(overrides). The makeFetchResponse includes both json() and text() methods on the fake Response, which is important since some error paths call .text() instead of .json(). For counts.test.js, a local mockCountsFetch() wrapper that composes both factories eliminated all 13 inline response shapes with a single function call per test.

## feedreader-7dq.31: spa-nav mock reduction
Option A (more integrative tests) was the right call. The module is a genuine orchestrator — it *should* call 6 different modules during navigation. Reducing mocks would mean testing DOM manipulation of real sidebar/views/counts modules, which adds coupling without real benefit. Instead, making each mock earn its assertions revealed that the test file was missing coverage for: popstate handling (reload on non-article pages, restore unread/starred), error handling (both navigateToNavRoute and restoreFromState catch blocks), route filtering (feed/category clicks correctly ignored), and DOM side effects (dropdown clearing, feed-action button hiding). The restoreFromState feed/category paths use dynamic import('./feeds.js') which is harder to test in isolation — would need vi.mock with dynamic import support.
## feedreader-7dq.39 + feedreader-7dq.40: Browser mode migration for drag-drop and pagination
Three critical limitations of Vitest browser mode v4.0.18 discovered:

1. **vi.mock() causes process hangs** — Module mocking in browser mode prevents clean process exit. Workaround: mock at fetch level with vi.spyOn(globalThis, 'fetch').

2. **vi.spyOn on ESM namespaces fails** — 'Cannot spy on export — Module namespace is not configurable in ESM'. Cannot intercept module-internal calls. Workaround: mock lower-level APIs (fetch, console) instead.

3. **Multi-file test execution hangs** — Running multiple browser test files causes process to never exit. Workaround: combine all browser unit tests into a single file.

Additional pattern: showToast() creates real 4-second setTimeout timers in browser mode. Track timer IDs in beforeEach and clearTimeout in afterEach to prevent timers from keeping the process alive.

location.reload() in browser mode actually reloads the iframe, crashing test execution. Use never-resolving fetch promises for success paths that call reload.

All tests consolidated in srv/static/modules/browser-unit.browser.test.js. Config: vitest.browser-unit.config.mjs. Make target: browser-unit-test.
## feedreader-7dq.37 + feedreader-7dq.38: Migrate article-actions and articles to browser mode
Key constraint: vi.mock() causes process hangs in Vitest browser mode v4.0.18, so only tests that DON'T need mocked dependencies can migrate. Tests asserting on IntersectionObserver creation/observation and window.scrollTo calls were good candidates — they only need real browser APIs + fetch spy. Tests asserting on mocked setPaginationState, updateCounts, etc. must stay in happy-dom.

Pattern: the browser tests verify real API instance types (toBeInstanceOf(IntersectionObserver)) which is impossible in happy-dom. The articles.test.js still needs MockIntersectionObserver in its beforeEach for the remaining renderArticles tests that call initAutoMarkRead internally but don't assert on IO behavior.
## feedreader-7dq.46: Lint test files with ESLint
ESLint config uses three config blocks: production files, unit test files (*.test.js), and browser test files (*.browser.test.js + tests/layout/*.test.js). Each block has appropriate globals. The old approach of a global ignores block has been replaced with file-scoped config blocks with explicit ignores. The restoreFromState feed/category paths use dynamic import('./feeds.js') which is harder to test in isolation — would need vi.mock with dynamic import support.
## feedreader-7dq.39 + feedreader-7dq.40: Browser mode migration for drag-drop and pagination
Three critical limitations of Vitest browser mode v4.0.18 discovered:

1. **vi.mock() causes process hangs** — Module mocking in browser mode prevents clean process exit. Workaround: mock at fetch level with vi.spyOn(globalThis, 'fetch').

2. **vi.spyOn on ESM namespaces fails** — 'Cannot spy on export — Module namespace is not configurable in ESM'. Cannot intercept module-internal calls. Workaround: mock lower-level APIs (fetch, console) instead.

3. **Multi-file test execution hangs** — Running multiple browser test files causes process to never exit. Workaround: combine all browser unit tests into a single file.

Additional pattern: showToast() creates real 4-second setTimeout timers in browser mode. Track timer IDs in beforeEach and clearTimeout in afterEach to prevent timers from keeping the process alive.

location.reload() in browser mode actually reloads the iframe, crashing test execution. Use never-resolving fetch promises for success paths that call reload.

All tests consolidated in srv/static/modules/browser-unit.browser.test.js. Config: vitest.browser-unit.config.mjs. Make target: browser-unit-test.
## feedreader-7dq.37 + feedreader-7dq.38: Migrate article-actions and articles to browser mode
Key constraint: vi.mock() causes process hangs in Vitest browser mode v4.0.18, so only tests that DON'T need mocked dependencies can migrate. Tests asserting on IntersectionObserver creation/observation and window.scrollTo calls were good candidates — they only need real browser APIs + fetch spy. Tests asserting on mocked setPaginationState, updateCounts, etc. must stay in happy-dom.

Pattern: the browser tests verify real API instance types (toBeInstanceOf(IntersectionObserver)) which is impossible in happy-dom. The articles.test.js still needs MockIntersectionObserver in its beforeEach for the remaining renderArticles tests that call initAutoMarkRead internally but don't assert on IO behavior.
## feedreader-7dq.46: Lint test files with ESLint
ESLint config uses three config blocks: production files, unit test files (*.test.js), and browser test files (*.browser.test.js + tests/layout/*.test.js). Each block has appropriate globals. The old approach of a global ignores block has been replaced with file-scoped config blocks with explicit ignores. The task description originally listed 16 errors but those were preemptively fixed by adding browser globals (File, AbortSignal, DOMException, PopStateEvent, Window) in the config task — only 6 unused-var warnings remained for the fix task.
## feedreader-7dq.43 + feedreader-7dq.45: setTimeout sleeps and PAGE_SIZE optimization
The innerHTML intercept pattern is useful for tests that trigger expensive DOM parsing but only assert on side effects (like pagination state). Walk the prototype chain with a while loop to find the innerHTML descriptor — avoids hardcoding Element/HTMLElement (which ESLint flags as undefined in unit test globals). The zero-delay setTimeout flush is always sufficient when all I/O is mocked — fire-and-forget async handlers resolve in the same microtask tick.
## feedreader-7dq.44: Reduce feeds.test.js runtime
Mocking api.js is the biggest win for test files that call functions which internally use api(). It eliminates the fetch wrapper overhead AND simplifies test setup (no Response factories, just data). The auto-mock from __mocks__/api.js provides a vi.fn() that can be configured per test.

Key insight: when using module-level vi.mock() for auto-mocks, vi.restoreAllMocks() does NOT clear call counts on vi.fn() instances created by the auto-mock. You must call vi.clearAllMocks() in beforeEach to prevent call count accumulation across tests.

The per-test happy-dom DOM parsing floor is ~2ms/test. For test files with 50+ DOM-heavy tests, the <100ms target is unrealistic unless tests share DOM setup or consolidate."}

## feedreader-7dq.21: Audit timestamps.test.js
Module is trivially simple (6 lines). Tests were already solid. Added invalid date edge case. No sub-epic needed.

## feedreader-7dq.22: Audit toast.test.js
Found and fixed 5 weaknesses directly (no sub-epic). Key issue: the dismiss button test was testing nothing — it clicked a button, switched to fake timers, then 'clicked again on a second toast' per comment but never created one, with zero assertions. The getContainer() recreation path (container removed from DOM) was untested despite having explicit logic for it.
## feedreader-7dq.1: Audit alerts.test.js
Found 15 test gaps, all fixable directly without a sub-epic. Main weakness pattern: every async function with a try/catch error path (console.error + showToast) was missing the error test. Also, initAlertsPage and initAlertDetailPage had untested delegated click handlers. Tests went from 30 to 45. The alerts module has good separation of concerns making it straightforward to test.
## feedreader-7dq.9: Audit icons.test.js
Pure constants module, tests were already solid. No changes needed.

## feedreader-7dq.23: Audit utils.test.js
Added 15 tests (22→37), no sub-epic needed. Main weakness pattern: missing boundary tests (exact-length thresholds in formatTimeAgo and truncateText) and missing edge case inputs (invalid dates, empty strings, non-string types). Note: happy-dom does not suppress script tag content via innerHTML like real browsers, so stripHtml script-stripping test was replaced with plain text test.
## feedreader-7dq.2: Audit api.test.js
Small module (26 lines) with 5 clear branches. Tests were decent but missed: JSON response without error field, empty error string (both fall back to 'Request failed'), network errors from fetch rejection, and explicit null data. Added 5 tests, no sub-epic needed.

## feedreader-7dq.6: Audit dropdown.test.js
Existing tests had good structural coverage but missed all keyboard activation paths (Enter/Space), several no-op edge cases (Escape with no open dropdown, Enter on non-menu-item), and the toggleDropdown first-item-focus behavior. Also the first describe block used .dropdown-btn class instead of .dropdown-toggle which meant those tests didn't exercise aria-expanded paths. Added 10 tests, no sub-epic needed.
## feedreader-7dq.10: Audit modal.test.js
Found 9 gaps, all fixed directly. Key patterns: untested early-return guards (null modal), untested default behavior when optional params omitted (focusEl), untested no-op paths (closeModal with no active modal, trapFocus with no focusable elements), and missing boundary condition coverage for trapFocus (Tab when not at last, Shift+Tab when not at first). The modal-content fallback in trapFocus was also untested.

## feedreader-7dq.15: Audit read-button.test.js
Found 5 gaps, all fixed directly. Existing tests verified title and innerHTML but missed aria-label. Also added toggle back-and-forth and truthy/falsy coercion tests. Module is very simple (16 lines of logic) so test coverage is now comprehensive.
## feedreader-7dq.11: Audit offline.test.js
Found 13 gaps, all fixed directly. Key constraint: _isStandalone is a module-level constant evaluated at import time, always false in happy-dom. This means handleOnlineStateChange, initOfflineSupport, and updateQueueCacheIfStandalone can only be tested for their early-return path. Main weakness pattern: replayPendingActions had many untested branches (non-matching messages, null data, empty actions, missing actions field, unknown action types, API failures). Also added round-trip integration test for disableNonQueueUI → enableAllUI.

## feedreader-7dq.12: Audit opml.test.js
Found 5 gaps, all fixed directly. Main weakness: success test didn't verify location.reload, headers, or FormData content. Error tests didn't verify reload was NOT called. Missing branches: no error field fallback, non-JSON response. Also added delegation tests (nested child click, negative guard). Found and fixed mock state leakage issue — needed vi.clearAllMocks() in beforeEach for module-level mocks.
## feedreader-7dq.25: Refactor _isStandalone
Key insight: vi.spyOn(module, 'fn').mockReturnValue() does NOT work for ESM internal calls — the internal code still calls the original function, not the spy. The correct approach is to mock the underlying browser API (window.matchMedia) that the function calls. Created a mockStandaloneMode() helper that mocks matchMedia to return {matches: true}.

## feedreader-7dq.3: Audit articles.test.js
Found 37 gaps, all fixed directly without a sub-epic. Main weakness patterns: (1) buildArticleCardHtml had many untested ternary branches (no-url, author, summary/content combos, image placeholder, sort-time fallback), (2) showHiddenArticles error path (showToast) was untested, (3) renderArticles didn't verify aria-busy, pagination state, or empty-list button visibility, (4) processEmbeds had 4 untested branches. Note: toast.js needed to be added as a vi.mock for error path testing.
## feedreader-7dq.4: Audit counts.test.js
Added 11 tests (14→25). Found mock state leakage: vi.restoreAllMocks() doesn't clear call counts on vi.fn() from vi.mock() — need vi.clearAllMocks() too. Key insight: the test file was missing a toast.js mock entirely, so the showToast error path was invisible. The alerts badge was completely untested. All aria-label attributes had zero coverage despite being accessibility-critical.

## feedreader-7dq.14: Audit queue.test.js
Added 4 tests (8→12). Module is tiny (39 lines). Notable: queueNext has no try/catch so API errors propagate — tested this explicitly. No sub-epic needed.
## feedreader-7dq.5: Audit drag-drop.test.js
Major gap: api.js and toast.js were not mocked at all, so the entire drop handler (both reorder and nesting paths) was untested. This is the most complex drag-and-drop module with async API calls, shift-key nesting, placeholder positioning, and cross-container sync. All branches now covered. The vi.waitFor() pattern works well for asserting on async drop handlers.

## feedreader-7dq.7: Audit feed-errors.test.js
Small module with good existing coverage. Main gap was no XSS escaping test despite escapeHtml being called. The banner-exists-outside-articles-view path was also untested. No complexity warranting a sub-epic.
## feedreader-7dq.16: Audit scraper-page.test.js
Major gap pattern: private functions called via initScraperPage (checkAiStatus, initAiForm, initManualForm) were completely untested — they have complex async flows with success/error/finally paths. modal.js was unmocked so openModal/closeModal calls were invisible. The initScraperPageListeners describe block uses a pattern of calling initScraperPageListeners() once at the describe level since listeners accumulate on document — this works well but means delegation tests share state.

## feedreader-7dq.17: Audit search.test.js
toast.js was imported by the source but not mocked in tests, so all error paths that call showToast were invisible. ## feedreader-7dq.24: Audit views.test.js

## feedreader-7dq.20: Audit sidebar.test.js

All success paths lacked location.reload() verification, meaning tests passed even if the reload never happened. Pattern: vi.mock('./modal.js') at module level is needed to verify modal lifecycle.

## feedreader-7dq.13: Audit pagination.test.js
Found 15 gaps, all fixed directly. Most critical: the entire error path (catch block with console.error + showToast) had zero coverage. The done-state transitions (empty articles → done, partial page → done, full page → not done) were unverified despite being core pagination logic. Found 11 gaps, all fixed directly. Also added boundary tests for getSetting (empty string vs undefined) and edge cases for applyHideEmptyFeeds (empty/NaN badge text).
## feedreader-7dq.26: Over-mocking audit
Cross-cutting findings from auditing all 27 test files (792 tests total):

**Duplication hotspots**: toast.js (13 identical mocks), api.js (7 identical mocks), and ~100 inline fetch response shapes. These are the highest-value targets for shared helpers.

**Three fetch strategies coexist**: vi.mock(api.js) vs vi.spyOn(globalThis,fetch) vs window.fetch=vi.fn(). The window.fetch assignment is worst (no auto-restore). Should standardize.

**Stateful mock anti-pattern**: settings-page.test.js reimplements settings.js internals. Since settings.js is pure localStorage logic, the real module should be used.

**Mock-heavy orchestrator tests**: spa-nav.test.js needs 6 mocks for 4 tests, suggesting it should either use fewer mocks (more integration-style) or the module should be refactored.

**Go tests are clean**: httptest.NewServer pattern, minimal mocking. No action needed.

Created 5 follow-up tasks (.27-.31) for actionable improvements.
## feedreader-7dq.27 + feedreader-7dq.30: Shared mocks and real settings
Key pattern: Vitest __mocks__/ auto-mocking works well for modules with a simple, stable API surface. Place files in __mocks__/module.js alongside the real modules, then test files just call vi.mock("./module.js") without a factory.

For settings-page.test.js, using importOriginal to get real getSetting while mocking saveSetting was the right split — getSetting is pure (reads window.__settings), saveSetting has side effects (fetch). Tests became simpler and more realistic.

Gotcha: __mocks__/ files need `import { vi } from "vitest"` at the top since they are loaded by the test runner.
## feedreader-7dq.28: Standardize fetch mocking
The three incompatible fetch mocking strategies (vi.mock api.js, vi.spyOn globalThis.fetch, window.fetch assignment) are now reduced to two: vi.mock for modules using api(), vi.spyOn for direct fetch callers. window.fetch = vi.fn() is eliminated. vi.stubGlobal('fetch') is also eliminated. Convention documented in srv/static/modules/TESTING.md.

## feedreader-7dq.32: Replace vi.waitFor polling in scraper-page.test.js
flushPromises() (setTimeout-based macrotask flush) is a reliable replacement for vi.waitFor() when all async operations use mocked functions that resolve immediately. Single flush per async step is sufficient. Runtime went from 756ms → 91ms.
## feedreader-7dq.33: Reduce pagination.test.js runtime
Key bottleneck was the PAGE_SIZE test rendering 50 full articles via buildArticleCardHtml + processEmbeds in happy-dom (~300ms). Mocking buildArticleCardHtml to return minimal HTML dropped it to 3ms. The other bottleneck (real 50ms setTimeout) was fixed with vi.advanceTimersByTimeAsync(0). Total: 411ms → 61ms. Approach: mock articles.js via auto-mock at module level, configure buildArticleCardHtml default in beforeEach to return HTML with article-card and article-content-preview classes, then override to minimal HTML in the PAGE_SIZE test.

## feedreader-7dq.34: Consolidate remaining inline vi.mock factories
Created 6 new auto-mocks. Key insight: ESM destructured imports (import { fn } from './mod.js') in source code DO get intercepted by vi.mock() because Vitest makes the module namespace mutable — the import binding is a live reference to the mock. So auto-mocks work correctly for intercepting calls in dependent modules. Two justified inline factories remain: settings-page.test.js (needs importOriginal for real getSetting), views.test.js (stateful store mock). The views.test.js mock could potentially be replaced by using real settings.js with localStorage (same approach as settings-page task .30), but was out of scope.

Created 3 factories in test-helpers.js: makeFetchResponse(data, {ok, status}), makeCountsResponse(overrides), makeArticle(overrides). For counts.test.js, a local mockCountsFetch() wrapper that composes both factories eliminated all 13 inline response shapes with a single function call per test.

Option A (more integrative tests) was the right call. The module is a genuine orchestrator — it *should* call 6 different modules during navigation. Instead, making each mock earn its assertions revealed that the test file was missing coverage for: popstate handling (reload on non-article pages, restore unread/starred), error handling (both navigateToNavRoute and restoreFromState catch blocks), route filtering (feed/category clicks correctly ignored), and DOM side effects (dropdown clearing, feed-action button hiding). The restoreFromState feed/category paths use dynamic import('./feeds.js') which is harder to test in isolation — would need vi.mock with dynamic import support.

3. **Multi-file test execution hangs** — Running multiple browser test files causes process to never exit. Workaround: combine all browser unit tests into a single file.

{"id":"feedreader-7dq.47.3","title":"Add mock export validation test","description":"Add a test that compares each __mocks__/*.js file's exports against the real module's exports and fails if they diverge. Prevents future mock drift.

Implementation: a single test file (e.g., srv/static/modules/__mocks__/mock-exports.test.js or tests/mock-validation.test.js) that:
1. For each file in __mocks__/, dynamically imports both the mock and the real module
2. Compares the set of export names (Object.keys)
3. Fails with a clear message if any export is missing from or extra in the mock

This catches structural drift only (missing/extra exports). Behavioral drift (wrong return values) is harder to detect automatically — that's what the custom mock review (feedreader-7dq.47.2) addresses.

Should run as part of 'make check' (include in the normal vitest config). Only needs to cover the remaining custom __mocks__/ files after feedreader-7dq.47.1 removes the redundant ones.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","owner":"exedev@lynx-fairy.exe.xyz","created_at":"2026-03-05T19:56:42Z","created_by":"exe.dev user","updated_at":"2026-03-05T19:56:42Z"}

Inline factory mocks in views.test.js and settings-page.test.js have intentional behavioral differences — views.test.js needs an in-memory store for round-trip testing, settings-page.test.js uses the real getSetting with window.__settings.

{"id":"feedreader-7dq.35.7","title":"Reuse Playwright page across header layout tests","description":"Each header layout test (header-wide, header-860, header-mobile) runs 7 tests for different feed name lengths. Each test opens a new Playwright page, navigates to the feed URL, waits for load, measures, and closes the page — 7 full page loads per file, 21 across the three header files.

Since the tests only vary the feed name between measurements, they can navigate once in `beforeAll`, then for each test just call `setFeedName` + reload or re-navigate within the same page. Better still, a custom command could:
1. Navigate once
2. Loop through all feed names, calling `setFeedName` via API, then `page.reload()` (cheaper than full `newPage` + `goto`)
3. Return all measurements in one batch

Alternatively, a simpler approach: add a custom command that takes a page, calls `setFeedName`, reloads, and re-measures — keeping the test structure but avoiding page open/close overhead.

The `measureLayoutMultiWidth` command already demonstrates page reuse across viewport widths. The same pattern applies here for feed name variations.

**Files:** `vitest.browser.config.mjs` (new/modified commands), `tests/layout/header-wide.test.js`, `tests/layout/header-860.test.js`, `tests/layout/header-mobile.test.js`","acceptance_criteria":"1. All 30 layout tests still pass with identical assertions
2. Total header test runtime reduced by at least 50%
3. No flaky failures from page reuse","status":"open","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","owner":"exedev@lynx-fairy.exe.xyz","created_at":"2026-03-05T15:58:00Z","created_by":"exe.dev user","updated_at":"2026-03-05T15:58:00Z"}

new_value: Added batch measurement commands (measureLayoutForNames, measureLayoutForNamesMultiWidth) that reuse a single Playwright page. Navigate once, then vary h1 text via page.evaluate() instead of reload. Header test runtime reduced ~59% (7500ms → 3050ms). All 30 tests pass with identical assertions.

{"id":"feedreader-lc7.4","title":"Configuration system: TOML config file with env var overrides","description":"Auth provider (and future app settings) are configured via a TOML config file with environment variable overrides.

**Config file**: loaded from a path specified by --config flag (default: config.toml). The setup UI writes this file.

**Env var overrides**: flat env vars override config file values when set. Convention: AUTH_PROVIDER, AUTH_CLOUDFLARE_TEAM_DOMAIN, etc. Env vars win over file values.

**Format**: TOML (Go-idiomatic, less error-prone than YAML).

**Example config.toml**:
```toml
[auth]
provider = "cloudflare"

[auth.cloudflare]
team_domain = "myteam"
audience = "abc123"

[auth.tailscale]
# no config needed

[auth.oauth2_proxy]
# uses default headers

[auth.authelia]
# uses default headers
```

**Existing CLI flags** (--listen, --db, --email-domain) migrate into the config file but remain as flags for backward compatibility. Flags override config file values.

**Precedence**: env vars > CLI flags > config file > defaults

**TOML library**: github.com/BurntSushi/toml (standard, well-maintained, pure Go).","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"decision","owner":"exedev@lynx-fairy.exe.xyz","created_at":"2026-03-06T02:57:34Z","created_by":"exe.dev user","updated_at":"2026-03-06T02:57:34Z"}

new_value: {"notes":"Decision: auto-create user on first login (current behavior preserved). No pre-registration required."}

CLI flags remain for backward compatibility and override config file values. Precedence: env vars \u003e CLI flags \u003e config file \u003e defaults."}

{"id":"feedreader-lc7.5","title":"Dockerfile and Docker image","description":"Create a Dockerfile for the feedreader app.

Multi-stage build:
1. Go builder stage — compiles the binary
2. Minimal runtime stage (alpine or distroless) — just the binary

Image should:
- Expose port 8000
- Accept config via /data/config.toml (or CONFIG_FILE env var)
- Store SQLite DB at /data/db.sqlite3 (or DB path from config)
- /data as a volume mount point for persistence
- Non-root user

Example usage:
```
docker run -p 8000:8000 -v feedreader-data:/data feedreader
```

{"id":"feedreader-lc7.7","title":"Newsletter ingestion via HTTP webhook","description":"Replace Maildir polling with an HTTP webhook endpoint for newsletter ingestion.

Add POST /api/newsletter/ingest that accepts incoming email (RFC 822 or parsed JSON, depending on sender format). The existing email parsing logic in srv/email/email.go is reused — just the delivery mechanism changes.

This is the primary/recommended newsletter delivery method. Pairs naturally with Cloudflare Email Routing for users already on Cloudflare Tunnel.

{"id":"feedreader-420","title":"Full JSON export/import for user data migration","description":"Add a full export/import mechanism that captures everything needed to recreate a user's setup on a new instance. This complements OPML (which only covers RSS feeds).

**Export** (GET /api/export or UI button):
JSON file containing:
- All feeds (url, name, feed_type, fetch_interval, content_filters, scraper_config, skip_retention)
- Folder structure and feed-to-folder assignments
- Scraper modules (name, description, script, script_type)
- Exclusion rules (per-folder keyword/author filters)
- User settings/preferences

Does NOT include: articles, read history, queue (too large, not portable).

**Import** (POST /api/import or UI upload):
Accepts the JSON export format. Creates feeds, folders, scraper modules, and exclusion rules. Skips duplicates (by URL for feeds, by name for scrapers).

**Feed types to support:**
- rss (217 feeds)
- huggingface (10)
- newsletter (4) — export email config
- scraper (1) — export with scraper_module reference and config
- youtube-playlist (1)

OPML export/import remains unchanged for interop with other readers.

This is a main-branch feature — useful for backup and for migrating to standalone deployments.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"feature","owner":"exedev@lynx-fairy.exe.xyz","created_at":"2026-03-06T03:21:47Z","created_by":"exe.dev user","updated_at":"2026-03-06T03:21:47Z"}

{"id":"feedreader-lc7.10.3","title":"docker-compose.yml for Caddy + Authelia + feedreader","description":"Create the Docker Compose file that wires up all three services.

File: deploy/docker-compose.yml

Services:
- caddy: official caddy:2 image, ports 80+443, mounts Caddyfile and caddy_data volume
- authelia: official authelia/authelia image, mounts config from deploy/authelia/
- feedreader: our image, AUTH_PROVIDER=authelia env var, feedreader_data volume at /data

Networking: all three on a shared internal network. Only Caddy exposes ports.
Volumes: caddy_data, authelia_data, feedreader_data (persistent).

Environment variables for easy customization:
- DOMAIN (used in Caddyfile)
- Any secrets Authelia needs

Keep it simple — a single docker compose up -d should start everything.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","owner":"exedev@lynx-fairy.exe.xyz","created_at":"2026-03-06T03:59:55Z","created_by":"exe.dev user","updated_at":"2026-03-06T03:59:55Z"}

{"id":"feedreader-lc7.10","title":"Docker Compose deployment with Caddy + Authelia (WebAuthn/passkey)","description":"Ship a turnkey Docker Compose deployment: Caddy (HTTPS/reverse proxy) + Authelia (WebAuthn passkey auth) + feedreader.

User setup:
1. Point domain at VM IP
2. Set domain name and create user in config
3. docker compose up -d
4. Register passkey on first login

Auth flow: passwordless WebAuthn passkeys (no passwords). Hardware security key as backup authenticator. Authelia's enable_passkey_login: true with one_factor access control.

Includes:
- docker-compose.yml (3 services: caddy, authelia, feedreader)
- Caddyfile template (forward_auth to Authelia, reverse_proxy to feedreader)
- Authelia config template (WebAuthn enabled, passkey login, minimal config)
- Authelia users template (one user entry)
- Setup instructions (README or DEPLOY.md)

Authelia injects Remote-User / Remote-Email headers — feedreader reads them via the Authelia auth provider (.2.5).

This is the recommended zero-config deployment path for GCP e2-micro or Oracle Cloud free tier VMs.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","owner":"exedev@lynx-fairy.exe.xyz","created_at":"2026-03-06T03:58:17Z","created_by":"exe.dev user","updated_at":"2026-03-06T03:58:17Z"}

{"id":"feedreader-420","title":"Full JSON export/import for user data migration","description":"Add a full export/import mechanism that captures everything needed to recreate a user's setup on a new instance. This complements OPML (which only covers RSS feeds).

**Export** (GET /api/export or UI button):
JSON file containing:
- All feeds (url, name, feed_type, fetch_interval, content_filters, scraper_config, skip_retention)
- Folder structure and feed-to-folder assignments
- Scraper modules (name, description, script, script_type)
- Exclusion rules (per-folder keyword/author filters)
- User settings/preferences

Does NOT include: articles, read history, queue (too large, not portable).

**Import** (POST /api/import or UI upload):
Accepts the JSON export format. Creates feeds, folders, scraper modules, and exclusion rules. Skips duplicates (by URL for feeds, by name for scrapers).

**Feed types to support:**
- rss (217 feeds)
- huggingface (10)
- newsletter (4) — export email config
- scraper (1) — export with scraper_module reference and config
- youtube-playlist (1)

OPML export/import remains unchanged for interop with other readers.

This is a main-branch feature — useful for backup and for migrating to standalone deployments.","notes":"Requirement: include new/updated tests for all changed code. make check must pass before closing.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"feature","owner":"exedev@lynx-fairy.exe.xyz","created_at":"2026-03-06T03:21:47Z","created_by":"exe.dev user","updated_at":"2026-03-06T03:23:45Z"}

{"id":"feedreader-lc7.2.7","title":"Auth provider interface design","description":"Thin AuthProvider interface. The app never handles login — the reverse proxy (Caddy + Authelia/etc.) owns the login flow. The app only reads identity headers.

Interface:
```go
type AuthProvider interface {
    Authenticate(r *http.Request) (*Identity, error)
}

type Identity struct {
    ExternalID string
    Email      string
}
```

Middleware behavior:
- Calls provider.Authenticate(r)
- If identity returned: proceed with existing user lookup/creation (GetOrCreateUser)
- If no identity (nil): return 401 for API routes, plain 'not authenticated' page for browser routes
- DEV=1 bypass remains for local development
- No login redirects in the app — proxy handles all of that

Each provider implementation just reads its specific headers and returns Identity.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"decision","owner":"exedev@lynx-fairy.exe.xyz","created_at":"2026-03-06T04:14:42Z","created_by":"exe.dev user","updated_at":"2026-03-06T04:14:48Z","closed_at":"2026-03-06T04:14:48Z","close_reason":"Decided: thin interface, app reads headers only, proxy owns login, 401 on missing headers, DEV=1 bypass stays"}

{"id":"feedreader-lc7.2.2","title":"Cloudflare Tunnel + Access auth provider","description":"Add a Cloudflare Access auth provider.

Headers:
- Cf-Access-Authenticated-User-Email — user email (used as identity)
- Cf-Access-Jwt-Assertion — JWT token

Cloudflare Access doesn't provide a stable user ID separate from email, so email is the identifier. JWT validation against the Cloudflare certs endpoint (https://<team>.cloudflareaccess.com/cdn-cgi/access/certs) should be supported to prevent header spoofing.

{"id":"feedreader-lc7.7","title":"Newsletter ingestion via HTTP webhook","description":"Replace Maildir polling with an HTTP webhook endpoint for newsletter ingestion.

Add POST /api/newsletter/ingest that accepts incoming email (RFC 822 or parsed JSON, depending on sender format). The existing email parsing logic in srv/email/email.go is reused — just the delivery mechanism changes.

Webhook should:
- Accept common email webhook formats (Cloudflare Email Workers, Mailgun, SendGrid, Postmark)
- Authenticate requests via a shared secret / HMAC signature (configurable in config.toml)

Config:
```toml
[newsletter]
enabled = true
webhook_secret = "some-secret"
```

This is the primary/recommended newsletter delivery method. Pairs naturally with Cloudflare Email Routing for users already on Cloudflare Tunnel.

{"id":"feedreader-lc7.8","title":"Newsletter ingestion via built-in SMTP server","description":"Add an optional built-in SMTP server for direct newsletter email delivery.

The app listens on a configurable port (default: 2525) and accepts incoming email directly. Reuses the existing email parsing logic in srv/email/email.go.

SMTP server should:
- Listen on a configurable port (not 25 by default — avoid permission issues)
- Accept mail for configured recipient addresses only
- Support STARTTLS (optional, configurable with cert/key paths)
- Be disabled by default — opt-in via config

Config:
```toml
[newsletter.smtp]
enabled = false
listen = ":2525"
tls_cert = ""
tls_key = ""
```

This is the secondary/advanced option for users who control their own DNS and infrastructure. Requires MX records pointing to the server.

{"id":"feedreader-lc7.5","title":"Dockerfile and Docker image","description":"Create a Dockerfile for the feedreader app.

Multi-stage build:
1. Go builder stage — compiles the binary
2. Minimal runtime stage (alpine or distroless) — just the binary

Image should:
- Expose port 8000
- Accept config via /data/config.toml (or CONFIG_FILE env var)
- Store SQLite DB at /data/db.sqlite3 (or DB path from config)
- /data as a volume mount point for persistence
- Non-root user

Example usage:
```
docker run -p 8000:8000 -v feedreader-data:/data feedreader
```

{"id":"feedreader-lc7.2.7","title":"Auth provider interface design","description":"Thin AuthProvider interface. The app never handles login — the reverse proxy (Caddy + Authelia/etc.) owns the login flow. The app only reads identity headers.

Interface:
```go
type AuthProvider interface {
    Authenticate(r *http.Request) (*Identity, error)
}

type Identity struct {
    ExternalID string
    Email      string
}
```

Middleware behavior:
- Calls provider.Authenticate(r)
- If identity returned: proceed with existing user lookup/creation (GetOrCreateUser)
- If no identity (nil): return 401 for API routes, plain 'not authenticated' page for browser routes
- DEV=1 bypass remains for local development
- No login redirects in the app — proxy handles all of that

Each provider implementation just reads its specific headers and returns Identity.","status":"deferred","priority":2,"issue_type":"decision","owner":"exedev@lynx-fairy.exe.xyz","created_at":"2026-03-06T04:14:42Z","created_by":"exe.dev user","updated_at":"2026-03-06T04:14:49Z"}

{"id":"feedreader-lc7.10.4","title":"Deployment guide (DEPLOY.md)","description":"Write a step-by-step deployment guide for the Caddy + Authelia + feedreader stack.

File: DEPLOY.md

Sections:
1. Prerequisites (domain, VM with Docker installed)
2. Quick start (clone, edit config, docker compose up)
3. Configuration — how to set domain, create user, generate secrets
4. Registering your passkey (first login flow)
5. Adding a hardware key as backup
6. Updating (pulling new images)
7. Backup and restore (where data lives, how to back up SQLite + volumes)
8. Troubleshooting (common issues: cert provisioning, passkey not working, etc.)

Target audience: someone comfortable with a terminal but not a sysadmin. No jargon without explanation.","status":"deferred","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","owner":"exedev@lynx-fairy.exe.xyz","created_at":"2026-03-06T04:00:03Z","created_by":"exe.dev user","updated_at":"2026-03-06T04:08:11Z"}

{"id":"feedreader-lc7.10","title":"Docker Compose deployment with Caddy + Authelia (WebAuthn/passkey)","description":"Ship a turnkey Docker Compose deployment: Caddy (HTTPS/reverse proxy) + Authelia (WebAuthn passkey auth) + feedreader.

User setup:
1. Point domain at VM IP
2. Set domain name and create user in config
3. docker compose up -d
4. Register passkey on first login

Auth flow: passwordless WebAuthn passkeys (no passwords). Hardware security key as backup authenticator. Authelia's enable_passkey_login: true with one_factor access control.

Includes:
- docker-compose.yml (3 services: caddy, authelia, feedreader)
- Caddyfile template (forward_auth to Authelia, reverse_proxy to feedreader)
- Authelia config template (WebAuthn enabled, passkey login, minimal config)
- Authelia users template (one user entry)
- Setup instructions (README or DEPLOY.md)

Authelia injects Remote-User / Remote-Email headers — feedreader reads them via the Authelia auth provider (.2.5).

This is the recommended zero-config deployment path for GCP e2-micro or Oracle Cloud free tier VMs.","status":"deferred","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","owner":"exedev@lynx-fairy.exe.xyz","created_at":"2026-03-06T03:58:17Z","created_by":"exe.dev user","updated_at":"2026-03-06T04:08:13Z"}

{"id":"feedreader-lc7.10.3","title":"docker-compose.yml for Caddy + Authelia + feedreader","description":"Create the Docker Compose file that wires up all three services.

File: deploy/docker-compose.yml

Services:
- caddy: official caddy:2 image, ports 80+443, mounts Caddyfile and caddy_data volume
- authelia: official authelia/authelia image, mounts config from deploy/authelia/
- feedreader: our image, AUTH_PROVIDER=authelia env var, feedreader_data volume at /data

Networking: all three on a shared internal network. Only Caddy exposes ports.
Volumes: caddy_data, authelia_data, feedreader_data (persistent).

Environment variables for easy customization:
- DOMAIN (used in Caddyfile)
- Any secrets Authelia needs

Keep it simple — a single docker compose up -d should start everything.","status":"deferred","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","owner":"exedev@lynx-fairy.exe.xyz","created_at":"2026-03-06T03:59:55Z","created_by":"exe.dev user","updated_at":"2026-03-06T04:08:12Z"}

{"id":"feedreader-lc7.2.1","title":"Refactor auth middleware to be provider-pluggable","description":"Refactor srv/auth.go so the auth provider is selectable at startup. Each provider maps its specific headers to the internal User struct (ID, Email). Add a provider interface and a flag/env var to select which provider is active. The DEV=1 bypass remains.

The login redirect (currently /__exe.dev/login) must also be provider-configurable or removed in favor of a 401.

This is the foundation — provider-specific tasks depend on it.","notes":"Decision: auto-create user on first login (current behavior preserved). No pre-registration required.
Requirement: include new/updated tests for all changed code. make check must pass before closing.
Decision: provider initialization via switch on cfg.Auth.Provider at startup. Simple factory pattern, no registry.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","owner":"exedev@lynx-fairy.exe.xyz","created_at":"2026-03-06T02:53:29Z","created_by":"exe.dev user","updated_at":"2026-03-06T04:34:07Z"}

new_value: Refactored auth middleware to be provider-pluggable. AuthProvider interface with Identity struct, three providers (ExeDevProvider, ProxyHeaderProvider, DevProvider). Server.AuthProvider field set at startup. Unauthenticated pages return 401 HTML instead of exe.dev-specific redirect. All tests pass including new provider unit tests and updated integration tests.
Interface design implemented in feedreader-lc7.2.1. AuthProvider interface with Authenticate(r) -> (*Identity, error), Identity struct with ExternalID and Email fields.

{"notes":"## feedreader-lc7.1: Remove Shelley scraper generator
Completed clean removal of all Shelley AI scraper code. 852 lines deleted across 13 files. Also removed AI-specific rate limiting from ratelimit.go since there are no AI endpoints left. The scraper page now shows only the manual config section (no tabs). All tests pass.
## feedreader-lc7.2.1: Refactor auth middleware to be provider-pluggable
Implemented AuthProvider interface in srv/auth.go. Three concrete providers: ExeDevProvider (backward compat), ProxyHeaderProvider (configurable headers for any reverse proxy), DevProvider (DEV=1 bypass). Server.AuthProvider field defaults to ExeDevProvider. Unauthenticated pages now return 401 HTML instead of redirecting to /__exe.dev/login. The DEV=1 fallback is preserved as a secondary check after the primary provider returns nil. The config system (lc7.4) was recorded as a decision but not yet implemented in code — provider selection is currently done by setting Server.AuthProvider directly. The config file integration will come with lc7.12 (TOML library) or a separate implementation task."}

Exported header constants for all three Tailscale headers (Login, Name, Profile-Pic) for use by config system later. No JWT validation — Tailscale authenticates at the transport layer. new_value: Added AutheliaProvider in srv/auth_authelia.go. Reads Remote-User (stable ID), Remote-Email, and exposes constants for Remote-Name and Remote-Groups headers. Unit tests for all header combinations plus middleware integration test. All checks pass.

{"notes":"## feedreader-lc7.1: Remove Shelley scraper generator
Completed clean removal of all Shelley AI scraper code. 852 lines deleted across 13 files. Also removed AI-specific rate limiting from ratelimit.go since there are no AI endpoints left. The scraper page now shows only the manual config section (no tabs). All tests pass.
## feedreader-lc7.2.1: Refactor auth middleware to be provider-pluggable
Implemented AuthProvider interface in srv/auth.go. Three concrete providers: ExeDevProvider (backward compat), ProxyHeaderProvider (configurable headers for any reverse proxy), DevProvider (DEV=1 bypass). Server.AuthProvider field defaults to ExeDevProvider. Unauthenticated pages now return 401 HTML instead of redirecting to /__exe.dev/login. The DEV=1 fallback is preserved as a secondary check after the primary provider returns nil. The config system (lc7.4) was recorded as a decision but not yet implemented in code — provider selection is currently done by setting Server.AuthProvider directly. The config file integration will come with lc7.12 (TOML library) or a separate implementation task.
## feedreader-lc7.2.3: Tailscale Serve/Funnel auth provider
Added TailscaleProvider in srv/auth_tailscale.go. Uses Tailscale-User-Login as both ExternalID and Email since Tailscale doesn't provide a separate stable user ID. Exported header constants for all three Tailscale headers (Login, Name, Profile-Pic) for use by config system later. No JWT validation — Tailscale authenticates at the transport layer. Pattern follows the same simple style as ExeDevProvider (value receiver, no config fields).
## feedreader-lc7.2.5: Authelia auth provider\nAdded AutheliaProvider following the same value-receiver pattern as TailscaleProvider. Remote-User is the stable identifier, Remote-Email provides email. Note: Authelia uses the same default header names as ProxyHeaderProvider (Remote-User, Remote-Email), so users could also just use ProxyHeaderProvider. The dedicated AutheliaProvider exists for clarity in config and to export the Authelia-specific Remote-Name and Remote-Groups header constants for potential future use (e.g., group-based access control)."}

## feedreader-lc7.2.1: Refactor auth middleware to be provider-pluggable
Implemented AuthProvider interface in srv/auth.go. Three concrete providers: ExeDevProvider (backward compat), ProxyHeaderProvider (configurable headers for any reverse proxy), DevProvider (DEV=1 bypass). The DEV=1 fallback is preserved as a secondary check after the primary provider returns nil. The config system (lc7.4) was recorded as a decision but not yet implemented in code — provider selection is currently done by setting Server.AuthProvider directly. ## feedreader-lc7.2.3: Tailscale Serve/Funnel auth provider
Added TailscaleProvider in srv/auth_tailscale.go. Uses Tailscale-User-Login as both ExternalID and Email since Tailscale doesn't provide a separate stable user ID. Exported header constants for all three Tailscale headers (Login, Name, Profile-Pic) for use by config system later. No JWT validation — Tailscale authenticates at the transport layer. Pattern follows the same simple style as ExeDevProvider (value receiver, no config fields).
## feedreader-lc7.2.5: Authelia auth provider\nAdded AutheliaProvider following the same value-receiver pattern as TailscaleProvider. - promptWriter helper wraps io.Writer for errcheck-clean interactive output
- Proxy config section is only emitted when non-default headers are used (clean TOML output)
- Subcommand detected before flag.Parse() to avoid flag conflicts
- initCmd accepts io.Reader/io.Writer for testability (no os.Stdin/Stdout dependency)
- The config package is now ready for use by the setup UI (lc7.2.6) and future --config flag integration in main.go
## feedreader-lc7.2.4: OAuth2 Proxy auth provider
Added OAuth2ProxyProvider following the same value-receiver pattern as Tailscale/Authelia. Uses X-Forwarded-Email as primary identifier (external ID), with X-Forwarded-User as fallback when email is absent. Exports header constants for all four OAuth2 Proxy headers (User, Email, Preferred-Username, Groups) for future config system use. Note: OAuth2 Proxy's default headers (X-Forwarded-User/Email) overlap with ProxyHeaderProvider when custom headers are configured, but the dedicated provider gives users a clear, named option in config."}
